sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Falls: assessment and prevention in older people and in people 50 and over at higher risk
sources.jurisdiction: England and Wales; requires UAE pathway adaptation
sources.population: People aged 65 years and over, and people aged 50 to 64 with one or more factors increasing fall risk.
sources.clinical_setting: Community settings including emergency departments and minor injury units, and hospital inpatient settings.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for asking details of a fall and for comprehensive clinician assessment of specified falls-risk domains in the covered age group. It does not cover every traumatic injury, younger adults or children, and it must not auto-score fall risk.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.1.1–1.1.5 — identifying people for falls assessment
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports asking about the details of a fall when a covered person presents after one and explicitly rejects automatic prediction-tool use.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.2.1–1.2.3 — comprehensive falls assessment
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician assessment of cardiovascular, cognition, delirium, diet/fluid, dizziness, footwear, function, gait/balance, hearing, long-term conditions, medicines, neurological, osteoporosis, continence and vision domains when relevant.

archive.title: Falls: assessment and prevention in older people and in people 50 and over at higher risk
archive.publisher: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
archive.jurisdiction: England and Wales; requires UAE pathway adaptation
archive.population: People aged 65 years and over, and people aged 50 to 64 with one or more factors increasing fall risk.
archive.setting: Community settings including emergency departments and minor injury units, and hospital inpatient settings.

exact_relevant_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.1.1–1.1.5 — identifying people for falls assessment
exact_relevant_sections.evidence_summary: Supports asking about the details of a fall when a covered person presents after one and explicitly rejects automatic prediction-tool use.

exact_relevant_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.2.1–1.2.3 — comprehensive falls assessment
exact_relevant_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician assessment of cardiovascular, cognition, delirium, diet/fluid, dizziness, footwear, function, gait/balance, hearing, long-term conditions, medicines, neurological, osteoporosis, continence and vision domains when relevant.

sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.population: Children and adults with non-complex fractures managed in emergency or orthopaedic services.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for selected non-complex fracture assessment, documentation, imaging, weight-bearing, information, and follow-up contexts; partial for a generic fracture-follow-up workflow and not validated for UAE pathways.

exact_sections.heading: Overview; recommendations 1.1 and 1.2
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Covers non-complex fracture assessment and follow-up, repeated pain assessment, definitive X-ray reporting, Ottawa ankle and foot rules, and selected scaphoid imaging.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.4.1–1.4.10 — ongoing orthopaedic management
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports selected orthopaedic follow-up, stability review, weight-bearing, cast or fixation context, and return review for non-improving symptoms in specified fracture populations.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.5.1–1.6.12 — documentation, information, and support
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Requires structured fracture documentation, image and report continuity, honest information, questions, treatment expectations, rehabilitation, mobilisation, weight-bearing, management-plan communication, and documentation.
